metadata.working_title: Distinguishing examples while building concepts in hippocampal and artificial networks

abstract_sketch.problem: The hippocampal subfield CA3 is thought to function as an autoassociative network that stores experiences as memories, receiving two distinct encodings of the same sensory information through the mossy fiber (MF) and perforant path (PP) pathways. The computational purpose of this dual-input architecture has not been firmly established.
abstract_sketch.gap: Previous models assume CA3 stores a single hybrid encoding per memory, leaving unexplained why CA3 would receive two inputs with systematically different statistics (sparse, decorrelated MF vs. dense, correlated PP) and how the theta oscillation leverages them.
abstract_sketch.approach: We model CA3 as a Hopfield-like network that independently stores both MF and PP encodings of each memory, with retrieval selected by an inhibitory threshold. We then test model predictions against publicly available CA3 place cell recordings from two datasets, and finally generalize the computational principle to artificial multilayer perceptrons via a novel HalfCorr loss function.
abstract_sketch.key_results: Sparse MF encodings maintain distinct example representations; dense PP encodings merge along shared features to build concept representations. Rat CA3 place cells show sparsity-corrected position information preferentially conveyed by sparse theta phases (p computed by two-tailed Wilcoxon signed-rank tests). W-maze analysis confirms sparser phases preferentially encode turn direction, and Bayesian population decoding is more accurate during sparse phases. In artificial networks, DeCorr improves set identification but harms digit classification, while HalfCorr (applied to half the final hidden layer) excels at both tasks simultaneously.
abstract_sketch.impact: Complementary correlated and decorrelated encodings in CA3 can be biologically implemented through paired pathways and retrieved across theta phases. This principle generalizes to artificial neural networks as a plug-and-play loss function that improves multitask learning.

sections.title: Introduction
content_bullets: Hippocampus and episodic memory; CA3 autoassociative network hypothesis
Dual input architecture to CA3: direct PP from EC (dense, ~4000 synapses) vs. indirect via DG and MF (sparse, ~50 synapses)
Sparsification in DG promotes decorrelation; MF encodings are sparse and decorrelated, PP dense and correlated
Open question: computational purpose of dual-input architecture; prior theories favor pattern separation during storage with single retrieved pattern
Our proposal: CA3 stores both MF and PP encodings per memory and retrieves either depending on inhibitory tone
Theta oscillation as the physiological threshold that selects between encodings across phases
Three-part program: model predictions (Figs 2-4), rat place cell analyses (Figs 5-7), artificial network generalization (Fig 8)

subsections.title: MF encodings remain distinct while PP encodings build concepts in our model for CA3
content_bullets: FashionMNIST images from sneakers, trousers, coats as memories
Binary autoencoder produces EC patterns with NEC=1024, aEC=0.1
Random binary connectivity EC->DG->MF and EC->PP; WTA enforces target densities aDG=0.005, aMF=0.02, aPP=0.2
Correlations within concepts: rhoEC=0.15, rhoDG=0.02, rhoMF=0.01, rhoPP=0.09; synapse counts lDG=205, lMF=8, lPP=205
Equation 1 connects presynaptic density/correlation to postsynaptic correlation via inverse complementary error function
Visualization pathway: feedforward network maps CA3 back to EC, then image via autoencoder decoder

subsections.title: A Hopfield-like CA3 model stores and retrieves both encodings
content_bullets: Linear combination q = xi*xiMF + (1-xi)*xiPP with relative PP strength zeta=0.1
Asynchronous Glauber dynamics with threshold theta and inverse temperature beta
theta' = 0.5 retrieves MF; theta' = 0 retrieves PP; beta=100
Up to 30 cues tested per network; cue inaccuracy 0.01
MF cues retrieve distinct MF examples at high theta; at low theta, PP examples merge into concept representations as example load increases
PP cues yield qualitatively similar behavior; network is agnostic to cue type; performs heteroassociation
Random pattern analysis: MF patterns density 0.01 correlation 0; PP patterns density 0.5; multiscale retrieval regime exists at intermediate loads with more correlated PP

subsections.title: Oscillating threshold enables alternation between examples and concepts
content_bullets: Four scenarios: baseline abrupt threshold, sinusoidal, alternative values (0.55/0.25 vs 0.6/0.2), cue-throughout
50 examples stored per concept across 3 concepts
20 cues tested in each of 20 networks, retrieval behavior summarized between cycles 60-120
Lower-amplitude oscillations disrupt alternation; stall on one encoding type

subsections.title: CA3 place cells convey more fine-scale position information during sparse theta phases
content_bullets: CRCNS hc-3 dataset (Mizuseki et al., 2013, 2014)
Place cell selection: phase-independent position information >0.5 using 1 cm bins
Phase bins 30 deg; min 8 spikes per phase value; theta modulation requires max/min active phase ratio >= 2
Fig 5L: 35 valid CA3 fields, 47 valid CA1 fields with 5 progress bins and min 5 spikes
Sparsity correction via 100 null-matched neurons; two-tailed Wilcoxon signed-rank tests; two-tailed Mann-Whitney U for precessing vs. other
CA3 place fields convey significantly more position information during sparse phases; CA1 does not
Precession threshold: score > 0.3, slope steeper than -72 deg/field

subsections.title: CA3 place cells convey more coarse-scale position information during dense theta phases
content_bullets: Coarse position binning across whole track; windows of 4 contiguous bins
Dense phases most preferentially informative at coarsest scales; trend tested by Spearman's rho
Valid CA3 neurons at scales 1/16, 1/8, 1/4: 47, 49, 56; CA1: 122, 137, 144
Shuffled data shows no dense/sparse difference, validating methodology

subsections.title: W-maze data confirm sparser theta phases encode example-like turn direction
content_bullets: CRCNS hc-6 dataset (Karlsson et al., 2015); 2 turn directions; phase bins 45 deg
Min 5 spikes per phase value; theta modulation threshold as before
99 valid CA3 neurons; 187 valid CA1 neurons; 8 valid CA3 sessions, 25 valid CA1 sessions for population decoding
Bayesian population decoding: sliding windows width 0.5 s, stride 0.25 s; min 2 spikes; min 5 simultaneously recorded neurons; rectify activity below 0.02 * max
Sparse phases convey more turn information per spike; higher population decoding confidence and accuracy during sparse phases; effects specific to CA3 (not CA1)

subsections.title: Complementary encodings improve multitask machine learning
content_bullets: MNIST digit classification (concept task) + random set identification with 20% pixel corruption (example task)
Single-task MLP: two hidden tanh layers 50 neurons; softmax output; SGD batch 50 lr 1e-4 momentum 0.9; >99.9% train accuracy
DeCorr loss decorrelates batch items across final hidden layer; epsilon=0.001; lambda=0 baseline, lambda=1 DeCorr
Baseline better at concept learning; DeCorr better at example learning; 32 networks each
Multitask MLP: two hidden tanh layers 100 neurons; 1000 train images; 10 sets; 100 epochs; 64 networks per condition
HalfCorr decorrelates only second half of final hidden layer: solves tradeoff and outperforms baseline and DeCorr in both tasks
Neuron influence via clamping: correlated neurons dominate concept learning, decorrelated dominate example learning

sections.title: Methods
content_bullets: Binary autoencoder: 3 hidden layers 128/1024/128; ReLU; sigmoid output; Heaviside binarization with straight-through estimator; KL sparsification lambda=10; Adam lr 1e-3 wd 1e-5; 150 epochs batch 64
EC->CA3 feedforward: WTA with l synapses; apost enforced; postsynaptic correlations independent of l
Hopfield storage: linear summation weighted by xi=0.1 PP strength; synaptic matrix with pattern subtraction
Retrieval: asynchronous Glauber dynamics; rescaled theta' and beta'; 10 update cycles; noisy cue inaccuracy 0.01
Overlap metric: m = (S.x - a*sum(S))/(a*N*(1-a)); theoretical concept max = sqrt(rho)
Sparsity correction with 100 null-matched neurons; Wilcoxon signed-rank tests; Mann-Whitney U; Spearman rho
Bayesian decoding likelihood from Poisson assumption

sections.title: Discussion
content_bullets: Multiplexed encoding via MF and PP pathways selected by theta-modulated inhibitory threshold
Compatibility with phase precession observations; CA3 encodes future more sharply, CA1 represents future more broadly
Connection to prior theoretical work: Tsodyks-Feigelman, Fontanari, Treves-Rolls, McClelland-Goddard, Kaifosh-Losonczy
Relationship to memory performance and theta power; storage vs. retrieval timing across theta
HalfCorr as functional heterogeneity within a single layer as alternative to modular networks
Future directions: spectrum of decorrelation strengths; continual and reinforcement learning; biological brains

table_plan.title: Training hyperparameters for artificial networks

table_plan.caption_sketch: Hyperparameters used in each neural network component.

figure_plan.caption: Schematic of hippocampal model pathways: EC to DG via random sparse connectivity, EC to CA3 via perforant path (PP), DG to CA3 via mossy fibers (MF).
Encoding statistics across regions: density a and within-concept correlation rho.
figure_plan.data_source: aDG=0.005, aMF=0.02, aPP=0.2; rhoEC=0.15, rhoDG=0.02, rhoMF=0.01, rhoPP=0.09

figure_plan.caption: Valid cell counts passing inclusion criteria at each analysis stage and track scale.
Multitask multilayer perceptron architecture with HalfCorr loss applied to the second half of the final hidden layer.
Theta-modulated threshold alternates the Hopfield-like CA3 network between sparse MF example retrieval and dense PP concept retrieval.
